step.title: Step 8
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 15 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 154, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT': 'Move player 3 pixels in that direction between adjacent 3x3 cells'}
- Object taxonomy: {'b': 'blue player (3x3 block, 8 pixels)', 'N': 'light green target (3x3 block)', 'W': 'white cell (3x3 block)', 'g': 'gray cell (3x3 block)', 'B': 'black wall/background', 'M': 'pink HUD bar (bottom row)', 'c': 'charcoal single-pixel object at (23,40)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_row': 'pink M-colored HUD/timer bar'}
- Win pattern: Reach the green N cell to complete the level
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 153 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 8.1x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
There's no path avoiding (40,34)! The only path from (16,16) to (46,46) goes through (40,34). But (40,34) causes GAME_OVER. This means my understanding of the game is wrong.
Let me reconsider. Maybe (40,34) is not a trap. Maybe the game over is caused by something else:
1. Step limit - maybe there's a maximum number of steps
2. Time limit - maybe the game has a timer
3. Visiting the same cell twice - maybe revisiting cells causes game over
4. The path is too long - 36 steps might be too many
Wait, let me look at the path through (40,34) more carefully:
(16, 16), (16, 19), (16, 22), (19, 22), (22, 22), (25, 22), (28, 22), (28, 25), (28, 28), (25, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)
This is 36 steps. But the game over happened at step 15 (reaching (40,34)). So it's not about the total path length.
Wait, maybe the issue is that the game over happened because the player visited a cell that was already visited? Let me check if the path revisits any cells... No, the path doesn't revisit any cells.
Actually, let me reconsider the game over. The first game over happened after 14 moves at (40,34). The second game over happened after 15 moves at (40,34). Both times the player was at (40,34).
But wait, the first path was different from the second path. Let me check:
First path (from earlier): started at (16,16), went through some cells, ended at (40,34) after 14 moves.
Second path: started at (22,40), went through some cells, ended at (40,34) after 15 moves.
Both ended at (40,34). So (40,34) seems to be the trigger.
But there's no path avoiding (40,34). So either:
1. The game is unsolvable (unlikely)
2. My cell graph is wrong
3. The game over is not caused by (40,34) but by something else
Let me reconsider the cell graph. Maybe I'm missing some cells or the adjacency is wrong.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the cells more carefully. The cell at (16,17) has color 'c' (charcoal) and only 1 pixel. This is not a regular cell. Maybe it's a special cell that the player can interact with.
Also, I notice that the player has 8 pixels, not 9. This might mean the player is not a full 3x3 block. Maybe the player is a 3x3 block with one pixel missing, which could be the charcoal pixel.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the game is not about navigating through the cells, but about something else. Maybe the player needs to collect items or avoid certain cells.
Actually, let me reconsider the game over condition. Maybe the game over is caused by:
1. Stepping on a gray cell (g)
2. Stepping on a white cell (W) after a certain number of steps
3. Some other condition
Let me look at the cells the player visited before game over:
First path: (16,16) → ... → (40,34)
Second path: (22,40) → ... → (40,34)
Both paths went through a mix of W and g cells. The game over happened at (40,34) which is W.
Wait, let me look at the path through (40,34) again:
... (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), ...
(40,31) is g, (40,34) is W, (43,34) is g. The pattern is g → W → g.
Hmm, let me think about this more. Maybe the issue is not about the cell color but about the position. (40,34) is at row 40, column 34. Maybe there's something special about this position.
Actually, I just realized something. The game over might not be caused by (40,34) itself, but by the fact that the player can't move further from (40,34). Let me check the neighbors of (40,34):
- (37,34): not in cells
- (43,34): g, exists
- (40,31): g, exists
- (40,37): not in cells
So (40,34) has two neighbors: (43,34) and (40,31). The player should be able to move to either. But the game over happened when the player was at (40,34).
Wait, maybe the game over is caused by the player being stuck? But (40,34) has two neighbors, so the player is not stuck.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the game has a step limit, and the game over is caused by exceeding the step limit. Let me check how many steps the player took before game over:
- First game over: 14 moves
- Second game over: 15 moves
These are different, so it's not a fixed step limit.
Actually, I wonder if the game over is caused by the player moving to a cell that doesn't exist. Let me check the first path more carefully.
From the first attempt, the path was computed from (16,16) but the player was actually at (22,40). So the first few moves might have been invalid, causing the player to move to unexpected positions.
Let me try a simpler approach: just try to move from (16,16) to (46,46) using the BFS path, but this time I'll execute the actions one at a time to see exactly where the player goes and when game over happens.
Actually, that would take too many actions. Let me think about this more carefully.
Wait, I just realized something important. The BFS path has 36 steps, but the game over happened at step 14-15. This means the game over is not at (40,34) in the BFS path, but at some earlier position.
Let me re-examine. The first game over:
- Player started at (16,16)
- Path was computed from (16,16) to (46,46)
- Game over at step 14, player at (40,34)
But the BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46) has 36 steps, and (40,34) is at position 27 in the path (0-indexed: 26). So after 14 moves, the player should be at position 14 in the path, which is (28,34), not (40,34).
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-examine the first path.
Actually, I think the issue is that the first path was computed incorrectly because the player was not at (16,16) but at (22,40). So the path was wrong from the start.
Let me focus on the second attempt. The player was at (22,40) and the path was:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), ...
After 15 moves, the player should be at (40,31), not (40,34). But the result said the player is at (40,34). This is confusing.
Wait, let me count the moves again:
Start: (22,40)
1. LEFT → (22,37)
2. DOWN → (25,37) - but (25,37) is not in cells!
Hmm, so the second move (DOWN) would try to go from (22,37) to (25,37), but (25,37) doesn't exist. So the player might not have moved, or moved to a different position.
Actually, I think the issue is that my cell graph is wrong. Let me check if (25,37) exists... Looking at the cells list, I don't see (25,37). So the player can't move DOWN from (22,37).
But the actions were executed, so something must have happened. Maybe the player stayed at (22,37) or moved to a different position.
I think the issue is that I'm not correctly modeling the game. Let me try a different approach: instead of computing a full path, let me try to move the player one step at a time and observe the result.
Actually, that would be too slow. Let me think about this more.
OK here's a new hypothesis: maybe the game over is caused by the player trying to move to a cell that doesn't exist (i.e., moving into a wall). In the first attempt, the path was computed from (16,16) but the player was at (22,40), so the first few moves were invalid and caused the player to move into walls, triggering game over.
In the second attempt, the path was computed from (22,40), but the adjacency might be wrong. Let me check:
From (22,40), LEFT goes to (22,37) which exists. From (22,37), DOWN goes to (25,37) which doesn't exist. So the second move would be invalid.
But wait, the path I computed was:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), ...
From (22,37), the next cell in the path is (22,34), which is LEFT, not DOWN. So the actions should be:
LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, ...
But the actions I executed were:
LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, ...
So the first action was LEFT (correct), but the second action was DOWN (incorrect, should be LEFT). This means the path I computed was wrong.
Let me re-examine the BFS path from (22,40) to (46,46):
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)
Actions:
(22,40) → (22,37): LEFT
(22,37) → (22,34): LEFT
(22,34) → (25,34): DOWN
(25,34) → (28,34): DOWN
(28,34) → (31,34): DOWN
(31,34) → (34,34): DOWN
(34,34) → (34,31): LEFT
(34,31) → (34,28): LEFT
(34,28) → (34,25): LEFT
(34,25) → (34,22): LEFT
(34,22) → (37,22): DOWN
(37,22) → (40,22): DOWN
(40,22) → (40,25): RIGHT
(40,25) → (40,28): RIGHT
(40,28) → (40,31): RIGHT
(40,31) → (40,34): RIGHT
(40,34) → (43,34): DOWN
(43,34) → (46,34): DOWN
(46,34) → (46,37): RIGHT
(46,37) → (46,40): RIGHT
(46,40) → (43,40): UP
(43,40) → (40,40): UP
(40,40) → (40,43): RIGHT
(40,43) → (40,46): RIGHT
(40,46) → (43,46): DOWN
(43,46) → (46,46): DOWN
So the actions should be:
LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
But I executed:
LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
The difference is:
- My path: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, ...
- Executed: LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, ...
So I executed LEFT, DOWN instead of LEFT, LEFT. This means the first two moves were wrong, and the player went to an invalid position, causing game over.
Wait, but the executed actions were from the previous turn's BFS, which was computed from (22,40). Let me check what the BFS from (22,40) produced...
Actually, I think the issue is that the BFS was computed correctly, but the actions were wrong. Let me re-examine.
The BFS path from (22,40) was:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), ...
Actions: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, ...
But the executed actions were: LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
This doesn't match. The executed actions have LEFT, DOWN as the first two, but the BFS says LEFT, LEFT.
I think there was a bug in the action generation. Let me recompute the BFS and actions carefully.
Actually, looking at the executed actions more carefully:
LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
This is 25 actions. But the BFS from (22,40) to (46,46) has 26 steps (27 positions). So the actions are off by one.
Wait, let me count the BFS path positions:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)
That's 27 positions, so 26 actions. But the executed actions have 25 actions. So one action is missing.
Hmm, I think the issue is that the BFS was computed in a previous turn and the actions were generated incorrectly. Let me just recompute the BFS from (16,16) to (46,46) and execute it carefully.
But wait, the BFS from (16,16) to (46,46) goes through (40,34), which causes game over. So I need to find a different path.
Actually, let me reconsider. The only path from (16,16) to (46,46) goes through (40,34). If (40,34) causes game over, then the level is unsolvable. But that can't be right.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe (40,34) doesn't cause game over. Maybe the game over is caused by something else, like:
1. Moving to an invalid cell (trying to move into a wall)
2. Exceeding a step limit
3. Some other condition
In the first two attempts, the game over happened when the player was at (40,34), but maybe that's just a coincidence. Maybe the game over was caused by an earlier invalid move.
Let me try a different approach: execute the BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46) carefully, making sure each move is valid. If the game over happens, I'll check which move caused it.
Actually, the BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46) is 36 steps, which is a lot. Let me first try a shorter path to see if the game mechanics work as expected.
Let me try moving from (16,16) to (16,19) (RIGHT) and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The only path goes through (40,34) which causes GAME_OVER.
# Need to reconsider - maybe game over is from invalid moves, not (40,34) itself.
# Let me verify by executing the correct BFS path from (16,16) carefully.

# BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46):
path = [(16, 16), (16, 19), (16, 22), (19, 22), (22, 22), (25, 22), (28, 22), (28, 25), (28, 28), (25, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)]

actions = []
for i in range(len(path)-1):
    dr = path[i+1][0] - path[i][0]
    dc = path[i+1][1] - path[i][1]
    if dr == 3: actions.append('DOWN')
    elif dr == -3: actions.append('UP')
    elif dc == 3: actions.append('RIGHT')
    elif dc == -3: actions.append('LEFT')

print(f"Total actions: {len(actions)}")
print(f"Actions: {actions}")

# Execute in batches to detect where game over happens
# First batch: first 10 moves
batch1 = actions[:10]
print(f"\nExecuting first {len(batch1)} actions: {batch1}")
result = action(batch1)
print(f"Result: game_over={result.get('game_over')}, done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check player position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        c = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Player at: ({r},{c}), expected: {path[10]}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Move player 3 pixels in that direction"}, "object_taxonomy": {"b": "blue player", "N": "light green target", "W": "white cell", "g": "gray cell", "B": "black wall", "M": "pink HUD", "c": "charcoal pixel"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "pink HUD"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at (16,16). Only path to (46,46) goes through (40,34). Previous game overs may have been from wrong actions, not (40,34) itself.", "goal_model": "Navigate from (16,16) to (46,46).", "recent_findings": ["Only path goes through (40,34)", "Previous game overs may have been from invalid moves"], "open_questions": ["Does (40,34) really cause game over, or was it invalid moves?"], "current_plan": "Execute correct BFS path in batches to find where game over occurs.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Wrong BFS paths from incorrect start positions"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
There's no path avoiding (40,34)! The only path from (16,16) to (46,46) goes through (40,34). But (40,34) causes GAME_OVER. This means my understanding of the game is wrong.
Let me reconsider. Maybe (40,34) is not a trap. Maybe the game over is caused by something else:
1. Step limit - maybe there's a maximum number of steps
2. Time limit - maybe the game has a timer
3. Visiting the same cell twice - maybe revisiting cells causes game over
4. The path is too long - 36 steps might be too many
Wait, let me look at the path through (40,34) more carefully:
(16, 16), (16, 19), (16, 22), (19, 22), (22, 22), (25, 22), (28, 22), (28, 25), (28, 28), (25, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)
This is 36 steps. But the game over happened at step 15 (reaching (40,34)). So it's not about the total path length.
Wait, maybe the issue is that the game over happened because the player visited a cell that was already visited? Let me check if the path revisits any cells... No, the path doesn't revisit any cells.
Actually, let me reconsider the game over. The first game over happened after 14 moves at (40,34). The second game over happened after 15 moves at (40,34). Both times the player was at (40,34).
But wait, the first path was different from the second path. Let me check:
First path (from earlier): started at (16,16), went through some cells, ended at (40,34) after 14 moves.
Second path: started at (22,40), went through some cells, ended at (40,34) after 15 moves.
Both ended at (40,34). So (40,34) seems to be the trigger.
But there's no path avoiding (40,34). So either:
1. The game is unsolvable (unlikely)
2. My cell graph is wrong
3. The game over is not caused by (40,34) but by something else
Let me reconsider the cell graph. Maybe I'm missing some cells or the adjacency is wrong.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the cells more carefully. The cell at (16,17) has color 'c' (charcoal) and only 1 pixel. This is not a regular cell. Maybe it's a special cell that the player can interact with.
Also, I notice that the player has 8 pixels, not 9. This might mean the player is not a full 3x3 block. Maybe the player is a 3x3 block with one pixel missing, which could be the charcoal pixel.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the game is not about navigating through the cells, but about something else. Maybe the player needs to collect items or avoid certain cells.
Actually, let me reconsider the game over condition. Maybe the game over is caused by:
1. Stepping on a gray cell (g)
2. Stepping on a white cell (W) after a certain number of steps
3. Some other condition
Let me look at the cells the player visited before game over:
First path: (16,16) → ... → (40,34)
Second path: (22,40) → ... → (40,34)
Both paths went through a mix of W and g cells. The game over happened at (40,34) which is W.
Wait, let me look at the path through (40,34) again:
... (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), ...
(40,31) is g, (40,34) is W, (43,34) is g. The pattern is g → W → g.
Hmm, let me think about this more. Maybe the issue is not about the cell color but about the position. (40,34) is at row 40, column 34. Maybe there's something special about this position.
Actually, I just realized something. The game over might not be caused by (40,34) itself, but by the fact that the player can't move further from (40,34). Let me check the neighbors of (40,34):
- (37,34): not in cells
- (43,34): g, exists
- (40,31): g, exists
- (40,37): not in cells
So (40,34) has two neighbors: (43,34) and (40,31). The player should be able to move to either. But the game over happened when the player was at (40,34).
Wait, maybe the game over is caused by the player being stuck? But (40,34) has two neighbors, so the player is not stuck.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the game has a step limit, and the game over is caused by exceeding the step limit. Let me check how many steps the player took before game over:
- First game over: 14 moves
- Second game over: 15 moves
These are different, so it's not a fixed step limit.
Actually, I wonder if the game over is caused by the player moving to a cell that doesn't exist. Let me check the first path more carefully.
From the first attempt, the path was computed from (16,16) but the player was actually at (22,40). So the first few moves might have been invalid, causing the player to move to unexpected positions.
Let me try a simpler approach: just try to move from (16,16) to (46,46) using the BFS path, but this time I'll execute the actions one at a time to see exactly where the player goes and when game over happens.
Actually, that would take too many actions. Let me think about this more carefully.
Wait, I just realized something important. The BFS path has 36 steps, but the game over happened at step 14-15. This means the game over is not at (40,34) in the BFS path, but at some earlier position.
Let me re-examine. The first game over:
- Player started at (16,16)
- Path was computed from (16,16) to (46,46)
- Game over at step 14, player at (40,34)
But the BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46) has 36 steps, and (40,34) is at position 27 in the path (0-indexed: 26). So after 14 moves, the player should be at position 14 in the path, which is (28,34), not (40,34).
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-examine the first path.
Actually, I think the issue is that the first path was computed incorrectly because the player was not at (16,16) but at (22,40). So the path was wrong from the start.
Let me focus on the second attempt. The player was at (22,40) and the path was:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), ...
After 15 moves, the player should be at (40,31), not (40,34). But the result said the player is at (40,34). This is confusing.
Wait, let me count the moves again:
Start: (22,40)
1. LEFT → (22,37)
2. DOWN → (25,37) - but (25,37) is not in cells!
Hmm, so the second move (DOWN) would try to go from (22,37) to (25,37), but (25,37) doesn't exist. So the player might not have moved, or moved to a different position.
Actually, I think the issue is that my cell graph is wrong. Let me check if (25,37) exists... Looking at the cells list, I don't see (25,37). So the player can't move DOWN from (22,37).
But the actions were executed, so something must have happened. Maybe the player stayed at (22,37) or moved to a different position.
I think the issue is that I'm not correctly modeling the game. Let me try a different approach: instead of computing a full path, let me try to move the player one step at a time and observe the result.
Actually, that would be too slow. Let me think about this more.
OK here's a new hypothesis: maybe the game over is caused by the player trying to move to a cell that doesn't exist (i.e., moving into a wall). In the first attempt, the path was computed from (16,16) but the player was at (22,40), so the first few moves were invalid and caused the player to move into walls, triggering game over.
In the second attempt, the path was computed from (22,40), but the adjacency might be wrong. Let me check:
From (22,40), LEFT goes to (22,37) which exists. From (22,37), DOWN goes to (25,37) which doesn't exist. So the second move would be invalid.
But wait, the path I computed was:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), ...
From (22,37), the next cell in the path is (22,34), which is LEFT, not DOWN. So the actions should be:
LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, ...
But the actions I executed were:
LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, ...
So the first action was LEFT (correct), but the second action was DOWN (incorrect, should be LEFT). This means the path I computed was wrong.
Let me re-examine the BFS path from (22,40) to (46,46):
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)
Actions:
(22,40) → (22,37): LEFT
(22,37) → (22,34): LEFT
(22,34) → (25,34): DOWN
(25,34) → (28,34): DOWN
(28,34) → (31,34): DOWN
(31,34) → (34,34): DOWN
(34,34) → (34,31): LEFT
(34,31) → (34,28): LEFT
(34,28) → (34,25): LEFT
(34,25) → (34,22): LEFT
(34,22) → (37,22): DOWN
(37,22) → (40,22): DOWN
(40,22) → (40,25): RIGHT
(40,25) → (40,28): RIGHT
(40,28) → (40,31): RIGHT
(40,31) → (40,34): RIGHT
(40,34) → (43,34): DOWN
(43,34) → (46,34): DOWN
(46,34) → (46,37): RIGHT
(46,37) → (46,40): RIGHT
(46,40) → (43,40): UP
(43,40) → (40,40): UP
(40,40) → (40,43): RIGHT
(40,43) → (40,46): RIGHT
(40,46) → (43,46): DOWN
(43,46) → (46,46): DOWN
So the actions should be:
LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
But I executed:
LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
The difference is:
- My path: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, ...
- Executed: LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, ...
So I executed LEFT, DOWN instead of LEFT, LEFT. This means the first two moves were wrong, and the player went to an invalid position, causing game over.
Wait, but the executed actions were from the previous turn's BFS, which was computed from (22,40). Let me check what the BFS from (22,40) produced...
Actually, I think the issue is that the BFS was computed correctly, but the actions were wrong. Let me re-examine.
The BFS path from (22,40) was:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), ...
Actions: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, ...
But the executed actions were: LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
This doesn't match. The executed actions have LEFT, DOWN as the first two, but the BFS says LEFT, LEFT.
I think there was a bug in the action generation. Let me recompute the BFS and actions carefully.
Actually, looking at the executed actions more carefully:
LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN
This is 25 actions. But the BFS from (22,40) to (46,46) has 26 steps (27 positions). So the actions are off by one.
Wait, let me count the BFS path positions:
(22, 40), (22, 37), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)
That's 27 positions, so 26 actions. But the executed actions have 25 actions. So one action is missing.
Hmm, I think the issue is that the BFS was computed in a previous turn and the actions were generated incorrectly. Let me just recompute the BFS from (16,16) to (46,46) and execute it carefully.
But wait, the BFS from (16,16) to (46,46) goes through (40,34), which causes game over. So I need to find a different path.
Actually, let me reconsider. The only path from (16,16) to (46,46) goes through (40,34). If (40,34) causes game over, then the level is unsolvable. But that can't be right.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe (40,34) doesn't cause game over. Maybe the game over is caused by something else, like:
1. Moving to an invalid cell (trying to move into a wall)
2. Exceeding a step limit
3. Some other condition
In the first two attempts, the game over happened when the player was at (40,34), but maybe that's just a coincidence. Maybe the game over was caused by an earlier invalid move.
Let me try a different approach: execute the BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46) carefully, making sure each move is valid. If the game over happens, I'll check which move caused it.
Actually, the BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46) is 36 steps, which is a lot. Let me first try a shorter path to see if the game mechanics work as expected.
Let me try moving from (16,16) to (16,19) (RIGHT) and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The only path goes through (40,34) which causes GAME_OVER.
# Need to reconsider - maybe game over is from invalid moves, not (40,34) itself.
# Let me verify by executing the correct BFS path from (16,16) carefully.

# BFS path from (16,16) to (46,46):
path = [(16, 16), (16, 19), (16, 22), (19, 22), (22, 22), (25, 22), (28, 22), (28, 25), (28, 28), (25, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)]

actions = []
for i in range(len(path)-1):
    dr = path[i+1][0] - path[i][0]
    dc = path[i+1][1] - path[i][1]
    if dr == 3: actions.append('DOWN')
    elif dr == -3: actions.append('UP')
    elif dc == 3: actions.append('RIGHT')
    elif dc == -3: actions.append('LEFT')

print(f"Total actions: {len(actions)}")
print(f"Actions: {actions}")

# Execute in batches to detect where game over happens
# First batch: first 10 moves
batch1 = actions[:10]
print(f"\nExecuting first {len(batch1)} actions: {batch1}")
result = action(batch1)
print(f"Result: game_over={result.get('game_over')}, done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check player position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        c = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Player at: ({r},{c}), expected: {path[10]}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Move player 3 pixels in that direction"}, "object_taxonomy": {"b": "blue player", "N": "light green target", "W": "white cell", "g": "gray cell", "B": "black wall", "M": "pink HUD", "c": "charcoal pixel"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "pink HUD"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at (16,16). Only path to (46,46) goes through (40,34). Previous game overs may have been from wrong actions, not (40,34) itself.", "goal_model": "Navigate from (16,16) to (46,46).", "recent_findings": ["Only path goes through (40,34)", "Previous game overs may have been from invalid moves"], "open_questions": ["Does (40,34) really cause game over, or was it invalid moves?"], "current_plan": "Execute correct BFS path in batches to find where game over occurs.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Wrong BFS paths from incorrect start positions"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
